pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

Personal Training Client Application Form

The more information you provide, the better I will be able to assist you. If you have
any questions with regards to this form, please do not hesitate to contact me. You
can use whichever measurement system (e.g. metric, imperial) you prefer to fill in
this form. Just state which unit you used (e.g. cm, kg, lb, inch).

 Full legal name: Chad
 Age: 35
 Height: 5'6
 Weight: 164.4 lbs
 Years of training experience: 22
For the section below, please give your current maximum abilities in weight times
reps, e.g. 250 lb x 6.
 Bench press: 225lbs x 1
 Squat (excluding body weight): 200 lbs front squat x 1 (same as before, due to
a previous back issues I haven't back squatted in a while, but will be starting
again this week)
 Chin-up (excluding body weight): 65 lbs max
 Overhead press: 155 lbs max

What are your fitness goals?

Current bodyweight is at 164.4 and bodyfat is at 17.3% excluding bone content
(attached to email is full dexa report.)
I'd like to improve my composition at the current weight and increase athletic
performance.
In terms of scale weight I'm not far off where I want to be, 161-162 is good for me and
for my gi and no-gi competition. However, my composition I'd like to improve a lot,
on a longer timeline.
When we first worked together, before we started the hydrostatic test had me
between 10-11%, and I was around 157.5. My conditioning felt fantastic, but I didn't
feel as strong on the mats or in the gym. So I'm not sure if it's feasible to be in the 10-
12% range on the dexa at 161-162?
Not sure if improving hormone levels will come along with that, or can be influenced.
That'd be a bonus. So my scale weight is almost there, Being between 161-162 works
for both gi and no gi, but I feel my composition and performance can improve.

BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

(despite my higher fat levels right now my conditioning feels pretty good, so it'll only
improve now).

How often in a week would you be prepared to train, if this was the optimal training
frequency?

Currently training 5-6 BJJ sessions per week, and 3 weight training sessions. This
seems to be a good training frequency and load where I see consistent strength gains
and consistent BJJ improvement.

What is your occupation? You may be general here, e.g. student/manager/personal
trainer/manual labor/doctor. The aim is to characterize your circadian rhythm,
stress and activity level.

I own my own business, we run an ad agency and run online traffic for various clients.
Sometimes I walk on my treadmill while doing my work (treadmill desk), but more
often than not I'm sitting.

Please list any pathologies and injuries (disabilities, allergies, illnesses, syndromes,
disorders etc.) you have or had:

Nothing severe, have had a history of various injuries and tweaks from sports. About
3-4 back pulls in my life, nothing life changing thankfully.

Right knee is anecdotally exhibiting some symptoms of worn meniscus. But my back
health has improved a great deal since we last worked together.

As you may know I had pneumonia and was hospitalized for 3 days about 6-7 weeks
ago. But currently I feel great, lungs feel good.

Do you plan to use any PEDs while under my supervision? If so, please list them:

No I do not. Although I'm young I have considered TRT however my current lab work
has shown a good improvement in hormone levels. My total, and especially free
testosterone levels used to be low for someone of my age, but the latest test I took in
April has shown a decent improvement. So I will continue to monitor.

Note that if you answer 'NO' to any of the questions below, I will not be able to take
you on as a client.

BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

 I declare that, while Menno Henselmans is my personal trainer, all the
substances I use are obtained and used in accordance with their governing
laws: YES

 I forego all rights to hold Menno Henselmans liable for any damages caused in
relation to following his advice as my trainer: YES

At this point, you may choose to return this form to me to obtain a price
quote before filling in the rest. Please return the filled in form to
mennohenselmans@bayesianbodybuilding.com and attach at least two full-body
pictures of yourself in clear lighting, preferably including a back double biceps and
front relaxed pose.

Activity level: please mark one of the fields below or delete the rest.

Other than training, I would say between sedentary and somewhat
active
 Sedentary (e.g. desk job)
 Somewhat active (e.g. doing groceries or walking your dog but otherwise
sedentary)
Stress level: please mark one of the fields below or delete the rest.
Average Stress
 Average stress (e.g. full-time work with deadlines and commuting)
If you answered yes to the question on PED usage, you can leave the section below
blank.
 Wrist circumference:
 Ankle circumference:
 How much did you weigh before you started lifting and what was your height
at that point? I began lifting when I was 13, I was around 115-120 lbs, and 5'4"
 Were you heavy as a baby? No, 7 lbs 3 oz
 Please attach a picture to this form in which the back of your right hand is
clearly visible. Ideally, place your right hand on a flat surface with your fingers
touching each other and then take a picture of it.

What are the lightest barbell weight plates you have access to? Microweights, below
1lb.

pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

What are the smallest weight increments between the sets of dumbbells you have
access to? 2.5 lbs

Do you have access to…
a squat cage or rack? YES
a 45° hyperextension bench? YES
a reverse hyper machine? No but I use a high box
a glute-ham raise? No but I use a barbell/rack to hold my feet in place and do them
on the ground
a standing calf raise machine? No
a dip/chin-up belt? YES
a leg curl machine and if so, is it seated, lying or standing? No
a pair of knee wraps? NO
gymnastic rings or a TRX? YES both
a seated calf raise where you push your body up (closed chain: see example image
below)? No

Please list all the supplements you are currently taking:

• 5 gr Creatine monohydrate daily
• 3 gr beta alanine
• 1 tablespoon liquid fish oil
• Curcumin extract
• Protein powder (whey/egg white mix)
• Garlic capsules
• Grape Seed extract for procyanidins source
• 4000-5000 iu vitamin D daily
• sometimes citrulline malate pre-workout
• 3-4 of my BJJ sessions per week I take caffeine in the form of coffee or powdered.
Not exceeding 100 mg.

Please describe or attach your current diet plan in detail:
headings: a squat cage or rack?     YES

pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

Currently I'm not really following a diet plan. I tend to eat low carb, unless I train. I'll
typically consume around 100-125 g of carbs immediately post workout, and maybe
20 g pre-workout. Basically I just tend to focus my carbs around my training.

Please describe or attach your current training program in detail:

Monday: 9 AM BJJ (30 minutes technique, 40-45 mins live grappling), 7 pm strength
training. Typically my strength sessions are compound movement based 19-23 sets
per workout.
Tuesday: 11 AM competition class BJJ training
Wednesday: 9 AM BJJ (30 minutes technique, 40-45 mins live grappling
Thursday: 11 AM competition class BJJ training, 8 pm strength training.
Friday: 9 AM BJJ (30 minutes technique, 40-45 mins live grappling)
Saturday: Strength training
Sunday: Typically rest

*** There is slight variance in this schedule. The main variable would be for example
I may do the competition training 3x a week vs. the 9 AM BJJ. But that's the main
difference. Strength work is at night typically, except Saturday.

***If possible, can you give me the layout for a day if I did strength training at 9 AM,
and BJJ at 6 PM. It's a rare occasion that this may happen***

Are there any times at which you are unable or unwilling to train? If so, please list
them:

The BJJ sessions are fixed times of course. But I am flexible on the strength training.
If you prefer me to do my strength training immediately following my BJJ, it's
possible. I typically won't do my strength work immediately before BJJ, however.

Please return the filled in form to mennohenselmans@bayesianbodybuilding.com
and attach at least two full-body pictures of yourself in clear lighting (without
excessive shadows), preferably including a back double biceps and front relaxed
pose. If you recently had blood work done, you can attach its results as well.

Here we have an intermediate-advanced mixed athlete performing at a high level in BJJ. This
is a typical case where a high carb diet is advisable. In fact, we found later with repeated
experimentation, that we could increase his energy intake significantly more on a high carb
diet than a more moderate carb diet, likely as the result of increased performance and
energy expenditure. Here's his weekly diet set-up.

training

morning

rest
Meal 1 is best consumed before your first training session, 8-
10 AM.
Meal 2 is best consumed after your first training session or 11
AM-1 PM.
Meal 3 is best consumed between 4-6
PM.
Meal 4 is best consumed after your evening training session
or 9-11 PM.
Your rest/cut days are protein sparing modified
fasts (PSMFs).
This means you should aim to minimize caloric
intake.
This effectively means your calories are maxima and your protein intake is a
minimum.
Don't worry about the carb:fat
ratio.
For satiety, it tends to help to either keep net carbs under 10 g per meal or to
minimize fat intake.

 Squat (excluding body weight): 200 lbs front squat x 1 (same as before, due to

(despite my higher fat levels right now my conditioning feels pretty good, so it'll only

Please describe or attach your current diet plan in detail:


--- Page 5 ---
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

Currently I'm not really following a diet plan. I tend to eat low carb, unless I train. I'll
typically consume around 100-125 g of carbs immediately post workout, and maybe
20 g pre-workout. Basically I just tend to focus my carbs around my training.

Please describe or attach your current training program in detail:

Monday: 9 AM BJJ (30 minutes technique, 40-45 mins live grappling), 7 pm strength
training. Typically my strength sessions are compound movement based 19-23 sets
per workout.
Tuesday: 11 AM competition class BJJ training
Wednesday: 9 AM BJJ (30 minutes technique, 40-45 mins live grappling
Thursday: 11 AM competition class BJJ training, 8 pm strength training.
Friday: 9 AM BJJ (30 minutes technique, 40-45 mins live grappling)
Saturday: Strength training
Sunday: Typically rest

*** There is slight variance in this schedule. The main variable would be for example
I may do the competition training 3x a week vs. the 9 AM BJJ. But that's the main
difference. Strength work is at night typically, except Saturday.

***If possible, can you give me the layout for a day if I did strength training at 9 AM,
and BJJ at 6 PM. It's a rare occasion that this may happen***

Are there any times at which you are unable or unwilling to train? If so, please list
them:

The BJJ sessions are fixed times of course. But I am flexible on the strength training.
If you prefer me to do my strength training immediately following my BJJ, it's
possible. I typically won't do my strength work immediately before BJJ, however.

Please return the filled in form to mennohenselmans@bayesianbodybuilding.com
and attach at least two full-body pictures of yourself in clear lighting (without
excessive shadows), preferably including a back double biceps and front relaxed
pose. If you recently had blood work done, you can attach its results as well.

Here we have an intermediate-advanced mixed athlete performing at a high level in BJJ. This
is a typical case where a high carb diet is advisable. In fact, we found later with repeated
experimentation, that we could increase his energy intake significantly more on a high carb
diet than a more moderate carb diet, likely as the result of increased performance and
energy expenditure. Here's his weekly diet set-up.
